Summary of what was found: Aluminium is used across packaging and electronics because it is light, protective, and associated with premium devices; electronics makers use aluminium because it is lighter than steel and signals premium quality.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: lightweight aluminium and recycled/circular aluminium logic. Original industry use case: electronics bodies, packaging, aerosol cans, foil, and closures. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: aluminium bottles, caps, outer refill shells, lipstick cases, compact shells, decorative sleeves, and mono-material aerosol/deodorant components. Weight/material reduction relevance: aluminium is light for transport and lighter than steel in electronics. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for premium/luxe shells and selective masstige applications where tactile feel and metal aesthetics matter. Limitations/caution: credible transfer route but should still pass price-first screening versus commodity plastics for mass-market beauty because it is a performance/premium material, not the cheapest solution.

Summary of what was found: BASF’s automotive examples show continuous-fiber thermoplastic composite structures can deliver large part-mass reductions: a seat pan 45% lighter and BASF formulations giving 30% savings in an oil pan and engine front cover.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: continuous-fiber-reinforced thermoplastic laminates and structural composite substitution. Original industry use case: structural automotive parts. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: highly engineered reusable outer shells, refill compacts, prestige closures, or durable refill frames where designers want structural stiffness at lower mass than metal-heavy parts. Weight/material reduction relevance: large weight-saving figures justify inclusion in the longlist. Premium versus mass-market fit: likely premium/luxe only. Limitations/caution: evidence comes from structural automotive applications, so this is an exploratory high-performance option rather than a near-term mass cosmetic route; screen early for cost, decoration complexity, and processing practicality.

Summary of what was found: SABIC’s STAMAX long-glass-fiber PP is positioned as a lightweight structural thermoplastic with high stiffness, impact resistance, structural performance, and easy flow for thin walls.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: long-glass-fiber reinforced polypropylene. Original industry use case: structural automotive parts, metal-replacement applications, and thin-wall components. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: non-product-contact refill shells, compact bases, overcaps, high-stiffness secondary components, or robust e-commerce-ready outer parts where standard PP may be too flexible. Weight/material reduction relevance: reinforced PP can substitute heavier or thicker structural parts while still processing into thin walls. Premium versus mass-market fit: more realistic for premium/luxe or selected high-abuse mass-market components than very low-cost disposable packs. Limitations/caution: property package is structural, so screen mainly for non-contact or high-stiffness parts, not as a universal bottle/jar resin.
Source name: STAMAX Resins: Automotive Thermoplastic Solutions — SABIC — https://www.sabic.com/en/industries/automotive/polymers/materials/stamax

Summary of what was found: SABIC says its automotive PP compounds feature low density, foamability for weight-out, stiffness via mineral or glass reinforcement, chemical resistance, and cost-efficient processing.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: lightweight PP compounds and foamed/reinforced PP families. Original industry use case: interior and exterior automotive polymers. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: closures, overcaps, compacts, outer jars/cases, refill housings, trays, and other non-contact rigid components. Weight/material reduction relevance: strong mass-market transfer route because the source combines low density, foamability, and cost-efficient processing. Premium versus mass-market fit: attractive for both, especially mass-market where PP’s cost/process profile matters. Limitations/caution: reinforced or foamed variants still need screening for aesthetics, decoration, and recyclability by pack architecture.

Summary of what was found: Trexel’s MuCell microcellular foaming technology reduces plastic-part density and reports part-weight reductions of 5%–40%, with raw-material savings and cycle-time benefits.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: microcellular foamed plastics. Original industry use case: automotive, aerospace, and consumer electronics. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: thick-walled injection-molded closures, overcaps, compact shells, refill cases, trays, pump collars, and accessory parts where material can be removed internally without switching resin families. Weight/material reduction relevance: direct quantified part-weight reduction and reduced material usage. Premium versus mass-market fit: strong for mass-market and premium if surface-quality standards can be met. Limitations/caution: requires package-specific qualification for visible-surface beauty parts, but it is a commercially proven weight-out route.

Summary of what was found: Trexel says its MuCell blow-molding technology can offer 20% weight reductions without compromising impact strength and product performance, with rapid implementation and no significant machine or die-head modifications.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: microcellular foaming in blow molding. Original industry use case: extension of foaming technology beyond automotive into blow-molded parts. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: blow-molded personal-care bottles, lightweight refill containers, and potentially tube-style or handled pack architectures where blow molding is used. Weight/material reduction relevance: directly transferable bottle/container process rather than only a structural molded-part process. Premium versus mass-market fit: especially attractive for large-volume mass-market personal care where double-digit material cuts compound across high unit volumes. Limitations/caution: validate top load, dispensing compatibility, decoration, and shelf appearance, but evidence makes it a high-priority shortlist candidate.
Source name: Trexel brings its MuCell blow molding technology to markets beyond traditional automotive applications — Trexel — https://trexel.com/trexel-brings-its-mucell-blow-molding-technology-to-markets-beyond-traditional-automotive-applications/

Summary of what was found: LyondellBasell’s BMW bumper example shows a PP foaming material that reduced vehicle weight while meeting requirements for scratch resistance, surface finish, performance, and aesthetics.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: foamed polypropylene for visible exterior parts. Original industry use case: automotive bumpers. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: visible PP outer shells, cap shrouds, refill cases, compacts, and durable overcaps requiring cosmetic-grade appearance and lighter weight. Weight/material reduction relevance: appearance-sensitive and performance-critical use case makes it relevant to beauty packaging, where finish matters. Premium versus mass-market fit: relevant to both, especially masstige/premium components where tactile and visual quality cannot collapse. Limitations/caution: packaging-specific tooling and finish trials are needed, but this source supports foamed PP beyond hidden industrial parts.
Source name: LYB helps BMW build a better bumper — LyondellBasell — https://www.lyondellbasell.com/en/media-insights/lyb-helps-bmw-build-a-better-bumper

Summary of what was found: LyondellBasell says its polypropylene compounds for exterior automotive use include low-density grades and support molding of large thin-walled components that contribute to part-weight reduction.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: low-density, high-flow PP compounds for thin-wall molding. Original industry use case: automotive exterior parts such as bumpers, mirrors, grilles, wheel covers, and trim. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: thin-wall jars, tray components, cap skirts, compact shells, secondary pack trays, and display parts where lower-mass polypropylene is preferable to ABS or heavier engineered plastics. Weight/material reduction relevance: high-flow, low-density PP can be used in large molded skins while reducing weight. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest near-term fit for mass-market beauty and personal care because PP is scalable. Limitations/caution: transfer should focus first on non-contact or compatibility-cleared parts, but the family is practical.

Summary of what was found: Covestro says its polycarbonate and PC-blend resins are used in consumer electronics housings and are suitable for thin-wall designs, with impact resistance, heat resistance, dimensional stability, and high-gloss/custom-color aesthetics.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: thin-wall polycarbonate and PC blends. Original industry use case: electronics housings, smart devices, security camera housings, and power-device enclosures. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: premium outer cases, clear caps, durable compact shells, device-like refill housings, and glass-replacement decorative shells. Weight/material reduction relevance: value comes from thin-wall capability plus premium appearance rather than lowest mass alone. Premium versus mass-market fit: primarily premium/luxe and device-packaging logic, not commodity body-care bottles. Limitations/caution: supports PC as a premium transfer route, not a universal substitute for PP/PE; focus on visual value-added and non-contact parts first.
Source name: Housing & insulation: Rugged polycarbonates for safer power applications — Covestro — https://solutions.covestro.com/en/highlights/articles/theme/applications/housing-and-insulation

Summary of what was found: SABIC’s consumer-electronics structural-solutions page shows thin-wall materials for phones, tablets, and laptops that can support design integration, process simplification, elimination of painting, and NCVM/PVD metallizing for metallic looks.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: thin-wall engineering plastics and metallizable electronics resins. Original industry use case: mobile-phone, tablet, and laptop housings/frames. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: metal-look compact cases, cap shrouds, lipstick shells, refillable outer packs, and prestige accessories where brands want a metallic effect without fully metal parts. Weight/material reduction relevance: can replace heavier metal shells or multi-step painted parts with lighter molded plastics plus thin metallic deposition. Premium versus mass-market fit: strongest for premium/luxe; selective masstige use if appearance value is high. Limitations/caution: screen carefully for recyclability and coating/finish compatibility because metallic-look plastics can add complexity.

Summary of what was found: Covestro and Dell used a Bayblend PC/ABS grade with 30% post-consumer recyclate in notebook parts while meeting demanding engineering specifications.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: recycled-content engineering plastics. Original industry use case: notebook components in closed-loop electronics recycling. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: recycled-content premium shells, refill cases, durable caps, or accessories where commodity PCR PP/PE is insufficient and stronger engineering-plastic aesthetics are desired. Weight/material reduction relevance: more about circularity than density reduction, but proves demanding visible/durable parts can use PCR in an engineered polymer system. Premium versus mass-market fit: premium/luxe or durable masstige, not entry-price packs. Limitations/caution: described grade is flame-retarded and mineral-filled for electronics, so it supports engineering feasibility but not automatic cosmetic-packaging compliance or contact use.

Summary of what was found: Avient launched a customized ABS formulation with metallic effect for electronics that helps eliminate painting while delivering scratch resistance, chemical resistance, and a metallic visual effect.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: pre-colored metallic-effect ABS. Original industry use case: consumer electronics housings. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: metal-look overcaps, compact cases, lipstick shells, and refillable outer components that currently use painted plastics or true metal cladding. Weight/material reduction relevance: relevant where brands use metal mainly for appearance because it offers a lighter molded-plastic path to a metal-like look. Premium versus mass-market fit: mainly premium/luxe and masstige. Limitations/caution: ABS should still be screened against PP/PE priorities and end-of-life strategy before broad use in cosmetics.
Source name: New Artisan ABS Thermoplastic Solution from Avient Features Metallic Effect for Electronics Brands — Avient — https://www.avient.com/news/new-artisan-abs-thermoplastic-solution-avient-features-metallic-effect-electronics-brands

Summary of what was found: Magnesium alloy is one of the lightest structural materials, but it is highly reactive and quick to corrode in vehicle environments, often requiring zinc, nickel, or chrome plating that is more challenging than plating many other substrates.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: magnesium alloy shells/components. Original industry use case: automotive wheels, brake systems, power steering parts, support brackets, and housings. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: extreme-lightweight prestige shells or accessories where very low mass is worth process complexity. Weight/material reduction relevance: magnesium belongs in the longlist as a true lightweight metal. Premium versus mass-market fit: specialty premium only. Limitations/caution: likely reject for most cosmetics because corrosion/plating challenges require coatings, finish control, and higher process complexity than standard beauty economics can support.
Source name: Demand for Plated Magnesium Alloys in the Automotive Market — AZoM / Hitachi High-Tech Analytical Science — https://www.azom.com/article.aspx?ArticleID=18272

Summary of what was found: Boeing states carbon-fiber composites in the 787 structure contribute to lower fuel usage, reduced maintenance costs, and longer range.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: carbon-fiber composites. Original industry use case: aerospace primary structures. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: limited-edition prestige shells, refill frames, or decorative structural accents where advanced-composite branding is part of the proposition. Weight/material reduction relevance: aerospace evidence confirms very high lightweighting potential. Premium versus mass-market fit: ultra-premium or special-edition only, if at all. Limitations/caution: aircraft-structure evidence supports carbon fiber as a performance benchmark rather than a realistic mainstream beauty-packaging route; include only to transparently filter out later.

Summary of what was found: ORNL says the greatest hurdle to wider carbon-fiber-composite use in transportation is the high cost of carbon fiber compared with other candidate materials.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: low-cost carbon-fiber development and commercialization limits. Original industry use case: transportation lightweighting. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: early rejection filter for mainstream beauty packaging and most premium formats unless an unusually strong refill/reuse business case exists. Weight/material reduction relevance: confirms why carbon fiber belongs in the scan but likely fails price-first screens. Premium versus mass-market fit: generally unsuitable for mass-market and selective even in premium. Limitations/caution: not packaging-specific but useful to prevent unrealistic recommendations.
Source name: Low Cost Carbon Fibre for Automotive Applications Part 1: Low Cost Carbon Fibre Development — Oak Ridge National Laboratory — https://www.ornl.gov/publication/low-cost-carbon-fibre-automotive-applications-part-1-low-cost-carbon-fibre-development

Summary of what was found: Smurfit’s honeycomb packaging is a paper-based solution with superior strength while being lightweight, designed to protect products and absorb shock during transport.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: honeycomb paperboard protective structures. Original industry use case: industrial/e-commerce protective packaging. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: e-commerce beauty protection, premium kit inserts, fragile pack buffers, protective trays, and secondary display/protection components that currently rely on EPS or heavier corrugated structures. Weight/material reduction relevance: strong route for tertiary and secondary packaging because it combines low weight and cushioning/protection. Premium versus mass-market fit: both, especially premium gift sets, fragrance, glass skincare, and e-commerce. Limitations/caution: check finish, dusting, and moisture/exposure suitability for each cosmetic pack architecture; as transport-protection material it is highly credible.
Source name: Honeycomb Packaging (Hexacomb) — Smurfit Westrock / Smurfit Kappa — https://www.smurfitkappa.com/products-and-services/packaging/hexacomb-packaging

Summary of what was found: Sonoco’s protective die-cuts and pads include fiber or chip sheets made with 100% uncoated recycled paperboard, plus corrugated die-cuts and pads for high-speed production.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: recycled-fiber die-cuts, pads, and corrugated protective inserts. Original industry use case: protective industrial packaging for retail and transit. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: replacement of foam/plastic inserts in gift sets, skincare kits, first-aid/personal-care bundles, transit pads for glass packs, and lightweight secondary/tertiary internal fitments. Weight/material reduction relevance: practical scaled fiber route for reducing plastic insert use while staying light and recyclable. Premium versus mass-market fit: broad fit across premium and mass-market. Limitations/caution: later screening should determine minimum caliper/ply needed for each fragility class; route is commercially mature and scalable.

Summary of what was found: Pregis says its hybrid air-cushion system is 99% air once inflated, does not add package weight in practice, and reduces transport burden because one pallet is equivalent to almost one-and-a-half truckloads of bubble.
Detailed description of what was found: Material/technology route covered: ultra-light inflatable cushioning. Original industry use case: inside-the-box industrial/e-commerce protection. Possible cosmetic transfer use case: tertiary protection for fragile beauty products, especially e-commerce shipments of glass fragrance, skincare sets, and premium samples/kits where transportation weight is critical. Weight/material reduction relevance: strongest weight-first tertiary route because the cushioning medium is mostly air. Premium versus mass-market fit: both, especially direct-to-consumer channels. Limitations/caution: film-based protective solution, so screen against paper/fiber routes where recyclability or plastic reduction is prioritized; on pure weight logic it is highly compelling.
Source name: AirSpeed Ascent Packaging System | Hybrid Cushioning — Pregis — https://uk.pregis.com/eu-solutions/by-need/inside-the-box-protection/cushioning-coiling/hybrid-cushioning/ascent-cfcc8761/
